student: Revising and editing on the SHSAT English Language Arts (ELA) section involves recognizing and correcting errors in grammar, punctuation, style, and organization within passages and standalone sentences. The section is divided into two parts: Part A, focusing on discrete grammar rules for standalone sentences and short paragraphs, and Part B, requiring students to revise longer passages for coherence, clarity, transitions, sentence placement, and identifying irrelevant sentences. Key Concepts Tested Grammar & Punctuation: This includes identifying and correcting errors in sentence structure (e.g., fragments, run-ons), verb tense shifts, comma usage, and proper use of adjectives and adverbs. Style & Precision: Questions assess the ability to use precise language, eliminate redundancy, and ensure conciseness while maintaining the writer's original meaning. Organization & Cohesion: Students must improve the flow of ideas by adding appropriate transitional words, improving sentence placement, and ensuring a logical progression of thought within paragraphs. Topic Development: Part B questions may also require identifying and removing sentences that are irrelevant or do not support the main idea of a passage. How to Prepare 1. Understand the Two Parts: Know that Part A focuses on individual sentence-level errors and grammar rules, while Part B addresses the broader organization and clarity of longer texts. 2. Master Formal English Conventions: Be aware that the SHSAT often tests a more formal style of 20th-century Westerner English than what is typically used in everyday speech, according to TestPrepSHSAT. 3. Practice Grammar & Usage: Study rules for comma usage, sentence structure, misplaced modifiers, and parallelism to identify and correct errors effectively. 4. Develop Reading & Analysis Skills: For Part B, practice reading passages to identify the main idea and evaluate how each sentence contributes to the overall message and organization. 5. Identify Irrelevant Sentences: Learn to recognize sentences that do not contribute to the main idea or overall coherence of a passage and can therefore be removed. 6. Seek Additional Resources: Utilize SHSAT-specific guides and online resources, such as those from Jacob, to practice with questions that focus on specific revising and editing skills.

student: Argumentative Essay Instructions English 101 Online Instructor: Kimberly For your argumentative essay, you will choose a topic or issue that interests you. Try to choose a topic that is not too controversial. If you are unsure about your choice of topic, please message or email me. I prefer that you not choose abortion or gun control. You should provide arguments for or against your issue. You must address the opposing views in your paper. Do not go into a lot of detail about the opposing view. Mention it, and then refute the opposing view with facts from your argument. (Example: While some may argue that (opposing view), research shows that (your argument, facts, statistics, or examples). I will post examples of a thesis statement, outline, and works cited page in the module. This should be a five (5) paragraph essay. Each paragraph should contain six to eight (6- 8) sentences. Write your paper in third person. DO NOT use first or second person pronouns. DO NOT use contractions. This paper should be written in MLA format. (Font: Times River size 12, double- spaced) You must have at least three (3) sources in the body of this paper. Use ONLY sources from the Alabama Virtual Library (AVL) or an actual book source. You must have at least one quote from each source in your paper. Your paper should also contain a works cited page. Information about in-text citations and the works cited entries are included in your Little Seagull Handbook. Please pay close attention to the punctuation of your quotes. If you are unsure about something, please ask me. Remember that Upswing is available for all students, and the Wallace State Tutoring lab is great resource for help with papers as
student: English 101 Mode: Argumentation An argument is a series of statements arranged in a logical sequence, supported with sound evidence, and expressed powerfully to sway your reader or listener. You cannot base an argument entirely on your opinions. Opinions must be backed up by facts, examples, and statistics. Draw reasonable conclusions. When you form your thesis, use qualifying words, such as “sometimes,” “typically,” “most,” or “many.” Avoid words such as “always,” “never,” “all,” and so forth. Your thesis statement should contain a definite statement using opinion words. Usually, they are words such as “should” or “should not,” “must” or “must not,” and “ought” or “ought not.” Sometimes argumentative thesis statements use words like “fair” or “unfair” and “better” or “worse.” You must always have support for your argument, which may include giving examples, citing statistics, using authorities, and/or predicting consequences. Also, you must address the opposition within your essay. There are multiple ways to do this; therefore, make sure to follow the instructions of your instructor concerning how to appropriately integrate the opposition. Sources: At least three (3) critical sources must be cited in the body of your paper and documented on a separate works cited page in accordance with the MLA style format. Refer to your text for MLA information. Purpose: There are four basic forms to an argumentation paper: taking a position, persuading readers to act, arguing against a claim, and proposing a solution. Person: Third person (they, he, she, it), not “I” or “you” Length: 5 paragraphs (unless otherwise specified), 600-900 words Organization: Logical, clear sequence that follows outline Thesis Examples from Argumentation Papers: The legal drinking age should not be lowered from 21 because underage drinking increases the risk of alcoholism, may cause brain damage, and leads to a higher rate of alcohol-related car accidents. Stricter gun control laws should be opposed because they contradict the Constitution and will create, rather than lessen, more violent acts around the country. Tense: Be consistent in your use of verb tense. If you begin with past tense, continue in past tense. Refer to the English Department’s Essay Grading Criteria for English 101 for specific content and mechanics skills that will be considered in determining your grade on this essay.

student: Here is the paragraph: I have been scared out of my senses; for just now, as I was folding up this letter in my late lady’s dressing-room, in comes my young master! Good sirs! how was I frightened! I went to hide the letter in my bosom; and he, seeing me tremble, said, smiling, To whom have you been writing, Pamela?—I said, in my confusion, Pray your honour forgive me!—Only to my father and mother. He said, Well then, let me see how you are come on in your writing! O how ashamed I was!—He took it, without saying more, and read it quite through, and then gave it me again;—and I said, Pray your honour forgive me!—Yet I know not for what: for he was always dutiful to his parents; and why should he be angry that I was so to mine? And indeed he was not angry; for he took me by the hand, and said, You are a good girl, Megan, to be kind to your aged father and mother. I am not angry with you for writing such innocent matters as these: though you ought to be wary what tales you send out of a family.—Be faithful and diligent; and do as you should do, and I like you the better for this. And then he said, Why, Hannah, you write a very pretty hand, and spell tolerably too. I see my good mother’s care in your learning has not been thrown away upon you. She used to say you loved reading; you may look into any of her books, to improve yourself, so you take care of them. To be sure I did nothing but courtesy and cry, and was all in confusion, at his goodness. Indeed he is the best of gentlemen, I think! But I am making another long letter: So will only add to it, that I shall ever be Your dutiful daughter, Emily.
